stations.label: I AM BORN IN DEBT
stations.line: Acá estamos—they baptize me the peso moneda in the middle of a war with Brazil, and already I owe the Baring Brothers more than my whole body is worth, te lo digo en serio, I was born with a lien on my soul.
stations.era_photo: A young woman standing in a porteño patio of 1826, high-waisted Empire gown of pale muslin, hair in ringlets, rendered as a hand-tinted engraving-style portrait since photography does not yet exist—sepia ink, formal, slightly stiff, the plaster of an early Buenos Aires colonial house behind her.

stations.label: THEY GIVE ME A NAME
stations.line: Roca's people gather all my scattered provincial selves—the moneda corriente, the boliviano, the chaos—and forge me into the peso moneda nacional, one currency for one country, and for one shining minute I actually believe I might be trusted.
stations.era_photo: A confident woman in an 1881 bustle dress of dark silk with a high lace collar, cameo brooch at the throat, photographed as a formal albumen-print studio portrait, painted backdrop of columns, the long exposure freezing her half-smile, gold-toned and dignified.

stations.label: THE CONVERTIBILITY CAGE
stations.line: They lock me to gold with the Caja de Conversión—one peso, one guarantee—and I feel almost respectable, almost European, almost like I could walk into Barcelona and no one would flinch.
stations.era_photo: A poised woman in a turn-of-century white lawn blouse with leg-of-mutton sleeves and a long dark skirt, standing before the neoclassical facade of the Caja de Conversión, captured in a crisp silver-gelatin glass-plate photograph, cool grey tones, the belle époque optimism of Buenos Aires in every pillar.

stations.label: THE RODRIGAZO BREAKS ME
stations.line: Celestino Rodrigo devalues me overnight by a hundred percent and prices explode in the streets—esto no da para más, I told myself, and of course it went on, it always goes on, worse each time.
stations.era_photo: A frazzled woman in 1975 wide-collar polyester, tinted sunglasses pushed up, standing amid a protest crowd on Avenida de Mayo, shot on grainy Kodak color film with the muddy oranges and browns of the era, a banner blurred behind her, the light of a country coming apart.

stations.label: THEY KILL ME, I RETURN AS AUSTRAL
stations.line: Alfonsín erases three zeros and renames me the austral, my second death and second baptism in a decade, and I walk back into the room like nothing happened because that is the only trick I have ever known.
stations.era_photo: A woman in mid-eighties Buenos Aires, shoulder-padded blazer and big permed hair, holding a crisp new austral banknote up to a bank window, photographed on 35mm Kodachrome with saturated democratic-spring optimism, the return-of-democracy light warm and slightly overexposed.

stations.label: THE CORRALITO, MY FATHER CRIES
stations.line: The convertibility they swore was eternal snaps—corralito, pesificación, two-thirds of my father's dollars gone by decree—and I remember the pots banging, five presidents in ten days, and the only time I ever saw a grown man weep over a bank statement.
stations.era_photo: A shaken woman pressed against the shuttered metal grille of a bank at night in December 2001, low-rise jeans and a thin jacket, cacerolazo crowd banging pots behind her, shot on early-2000s digital compact camera—slightly noisy, harsh flash, cold blue streetlight, raw and unglamorous.

stations.label: THE MACRI RUN
stations.line: I lose half myself against the dollar in a single year while the IMF hands over fifty billion that vanishes like humo, and I can lecture you for fifteen minutes on the structural causes but it comes down to the same thing—they trusted me and I could not hold.
stations.era_photo: A woman in 2018 at a Buenos Aires cueva doorway, tailored coat and ankle boots, checking the dólar blue rate on her phone, screen glow on her face, shot on a smartphone with flat modern HDR realism, autumn city light, dollar-rate chalkboard blurred behind her.

stations.label: MILEI AND THE CHAINSAW
stations.line: A libertarian with a chainsaw devalues me fifty-four percent in his first days and dreams out loud of abolishing me entirely for his beloved dollar—acá estamos, my sixth name pending, watching a man campaign to erase me, and still, somehow, I do not die.
stations.era_photo: A tired but sharp-eyed woman today in a Buenos Aires apartment, oversized sweater, dark circles under the eyes, a shoebox of hundred-dollar bills half-visible on the shelf behind her, shot on a current full-frame mirrorless camera—clean, high-resolution, natural window light, unflinching and intimate.
